summary.narrative: Governor Noem fulfilled several key campaign promises including significant tax cuts and maintaining South Dakota's business-friendly environment without implementing an income tax. Her tenure was cut short when she resigned in January 2025 to join the Trump administration, leaving some initiatives incomplete.

promises.text: Return $100 million in tax cuts to South Dakota taxpayers
promises.evidence: In 2023, Noem signed legislation cutting the state sales tax from 4.5% to 4.2%, delivering on her 2022 victory speech commitment to return tax relief to residents.

promises.text: Keep South Dakota free of a state income tax
promises.evidence: Throughout her tenure, South Dakota maintained its status as one of the few states without a personal or corporate income tax.

promises.text: Protect the unborn and advance pro-life policies
promises.evidence: Noem proclaimed 2024 the 'Freedom for Life Year' and promoted anti-abortion laws. Following the Dobbs decision, South Dakota's trigger law banning most abortions took effect.

promises.text: Keep South Dakota open for business and economic opportunity
promises.evidence: Noem maintained South Dakota as 'Open for Opportunity,' notably keeping the state open during COVID-19 and attracting business investment throughout her tenure.

promises.text: Reduce government regulation and red tape
promises.evidence: Noem signed multiple executive orders and legislation aimed at reducing regulatory burdens on South Dakota businesses during her administration.

promises.text: Serve a full second term as Governor
promises.evidence: Noem resigned as governor in January 2025 to become Secretary of Homeland Security, leaving approximately three years remaining in her second term.

promises.text: Expand broadband access across rural South Dakota
promises.evidence: While some federal funds were allocated for broadband expansion, full implementation of rural connectivity goals remained incomplete when Noem left office.
